
---
title: "Documentation for imfcharts"
date: "today"
author:
  - name: Datatools Team
    email: datatools-support@imf.org
    url: https://intlmonetaryfund.sharepoint.com/sites/fw-datatools
    department: 
      - name: ITD
      - url: http://datatools/
bibliography: references.bib
number-sections: true
code-block-bg: "#F0F0F0"
format:
  html:
    # styles.css is needed to scale down figure sizes.
    # Specifying any of
    # #| out-width: "25%"
    # #| fig-width: 8
    # #| fig-height: 5
    # for individual figures does not have any effect.
    css: styles.css
    link-external-newwindow: true
    code-fold: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    toc-float: true
    toc-location: left
    theme: custom-theme.scss # cyborg
    grid:
      body-width: 1200px  # Main body width
      margin-width: 150px # width of toc
      sidebar-width: 300px # left side
#  docx:
#    toc: true
#    number-sections: true
#    execute:
#      echo: false
#
#  pdf:
#    documentclass: report
#    papersize: letter

# Adjust title font
include-in-header:
  - text: |
      <style>
        .title {
          font-size: 48px; /* Adjust the pixel value as needed */
          color: #4B82AD;    /* Optional: change the color */
        }
      </style>
jupyter: python3
---

<!-- 2025-11-20                                             -->
<!--                                                        -->
<!-- Documentation on imfcharts.                            -->
<!-- To generate this document run                          -->
<!-- quarto render imfcharts.qmd                            -->
<!--                                                        -->
<!-- Geneating PDF output will open a Carbon Black window   -->
<!-- but ignoring it will render the document.              -->

```{python}
#| label: py-imports
import os
import sys

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib

# Set figure sizes
plt.rcParams["figure.figsize"] = (4.74, 3)

# Tables in quarto
from itables import show
from IPython.display import Markdown

import imf_datatools

from imfcharts import *
set_style('imf-articleiv')
```

<!------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------>
# Introduction {#sec-intro}
This is the documentation for the `imfcharts` package.
The goal of this package is simple, to allow easy (re)production of IMF charts for publications.
The technology to do this has been available for decades, our goal is to make this as simple as it should be.

The authors wish to thank the [Process Modernization Challenge](https://imf.brightidea.com/Process){target="_blank"}
committee for their support and encouragement. The project started out as an [idea](https://imf.brightidea.com/D1580){target="_blank"}
for the Challenge, and in Feb 2025 the project [received First Place in the Challenge event](https://imf.brightidea.com/Process){target="_blank"}.

The documentation is orgnaized as follows. In the next subsections @sec-quickstart is a Quick Start,
and motivation, examples are provided in @sec-motivation. The sections that follow are @sec-basics, followed by @sec-styles, and
integration with other systems are shown in @sec-R, @sec-stata and @sec-aida.

## Quick Start and Installation {#sec-quickstart}

The library is written in the Python language, but can be called from R (and perhaps in the future) Stata.
In all cases the Python library must be installed. This library is based on the well-known [Matplotlib](https://matplotlib.org/){target="_blank"}
library which is the basis for most Python visualization libraries.
Beyond Matplotlib the only dependencies are `numpy` and `pandas` so the library should be usable in any environment.

To install the library,

1. Install Python from the Software Center

2. Copy the folder `\\ecnswn12p\ems_shared\pub\datatools\dev\imfcharts` to your computer's `C:\ProgramData\Python3\Lib\site-packages` folder.

3. Open Command Prompt and run the following:
    a. `ipython` Open ipython session
    b. `from imfcharts import *` If no error message appears the installation is complete

Note that importing the library should create a copy of style files in your computer's `C:\User\[username]\.matplotlib\stylelib` folder.

To create a chart, prepare a `pandas.DataFrame` with your data and set the index to be what you want your x-axis to be.
For example, in most cases a time series is plotted, so the index should be set to the dates.

A simple example is given below.

```{python}
#| label: quickstart
#| code-fold: show
#| fig-cap: "Simple GDP plot."

import pandas as pd
from imfcharts import *

# Create data
data = {'dates' : ['2024Q1', '2024Q2', '2024Q3', '2024Q4', '2025Q1'],
        'Consumption' : [2.5, 3, 2.8, 2.7, 1.3],
	'Gov' : [1.2, 1.1, 0.8, 0.3, -0.2],
	'Net exports' : [-0.5, 0.3, 1.2, 0.3, -1.5],
	'Fixed capital formation' : [0.2, 0.3, -0.2, -0.3, -0.3]}
df = pd.DataFrame(data).set_index('dates')
df['GDP'] = df.sum(axis=1)

# Get all columns except "GDP" for bars
barcols = list(df.columns)
barcols.remove('GDP')

# Create chart
chart = Chart(df, linecols='GDP', barcols=barcols)

# Show chart
chart
```

As can be seen, the code needed to generate the chart is one line,


```
chart = Chart(df, linecols='GDP', barcols=barcols)
```

The chart automatically uses the default IMF Article IV colors, which is set by the library as the default colors.
Adding a few options will make the chart presentable:

```{python}
#| label: quickstart-better
#| code-fold: show
#| fig-cap: "Modified GDP plot."

chart = Chart(df, linecols='GDP', barcols=barcols,
              attrs={'GDP' : {'color' : 'black'}},
              title='GDP by Composition', subtitle='(Seasonally Adjusted)',
              yrange=[-3, 5],
              legend_bottom=0.1, ncol_legend=2)
chart
```

The differences with the first chart are:

- Added title, subtitle
- Specifid line for "GDP" to be black
- Specified y-axis range
- Moved legend bottom, specified number of columns in legend

All of these options and more are explained in @sec-basics.
The `chart` object can then be saved with commands

```{python}
#| label: quickstart-save
#| code-fold: show
#| eval: false

chart.save('GDP.pdf') # change extention to png etc.
chart.show()
```

The `chart` variable is a `Chart` object within the library, and provides a direct interface with the underlying
`matplotlib.pyplot.Figure` and `matplotlib.pyplot.Axes` objects so that they can be modified further.
For example,  the popular [`seaborn`](https://seaborn.pydata.org/){target="_blank"} library is built on top of `Matplotlib`
so it can be called on the created `Chart`.

<!-- TODO: Add example -->

## Motivation {#sec-motivation}

The Fund wastes enormous time and effort by not provding a standard program to generate charts[^chart-programs],
and this hurts in the Fund in multiple ways.

1. People (especially young ones) are forced to repeat menial tasks ad nauseam
2. The mindset is stuck in Excel and people do not understand that technology can mitigate these efforts
3. The standards of visualization are stuck with outdated standards and terrible aesthetics

[^chart-programs]: The ITD CATS team provides the Excel PCF tool for this purpose, but this is still contained within Excel. Furthermore, it has not been updated for many years.

Therefore goals of this project are:

1. Provide a package to easily generate IMF charts
2. Provide templates that allow users to easily switch to a new style

This allows Fund users (especially young ones) to realize that menial tasks can be automated and done with much less effort.
It also incentivizes the Fund to move to better standards of visualization since the transition is now much easier and
does not require editing every single Excel chart that has been created previously.

While the main library we provide is written in Python, it can be used from R (and in the future possibly from Stata),
and  given the recent advances in AI, it may be possible to use these tools through AI, see @sec-aida which will broaden the audience further.

For users of Python and R, combining with the [datatools](http://datatools){target="_blank"} now provides an end-to-end solution for

1. data collection
2. data transformation
3. visualizatoin

all in very accessible libraries. This allows **fully reproducible and automated workstreams**
which should greatly reduce the need to redo simple tasks over and over.

Below are several examples along with the code used to generate them.

```{python}
#| label: fig_lines
#| fig-cap: "Simple line chart."

# Get data from Haver (need imf_datatools)
col1, col2 = 'Unemployment rate', 'Combined rate of underemployment and unemployment (rhs)'
dict_series = {'S186ELUR@EMERGEMA' : col1,
	       'S186EUDR@EMERGEMA' : col2
               }
df = None
for series in dict_series:
    _df = imf_datatools.get_haver_data(series)
    _df.columns = [dict_series[series]]
    # Merge
    if df is None:
        df = _df
    else:
        df = df.merge(_df, left_index=True, right_index=True, how='outer')

# Create Chart object
chart = Chart(df, linecols=df.columns[0], rlinecols=df.columns[1],
               title='Labor Utilization', subtitle='(Percent)',
               xrange='2023-01:2025-08',
               yrange=[8, 11],
               )
chart
```

```{python}
#| label: fig_lines_bars
#| fig-cap: "Chart with bars on left y-axis, lines on right y-axis."

# Get data
dict_series = {'N186FPAP@EMERGEMA' : 'Return to asset',
               'N186FNIE@EMERGEMA' : 'Return to equity'}
df = None
for series in dict_series:
    _df = imf_datatools.get_haver_data(series)
    _df.columns = [dict_series[series]]
    if df is None:
        df = _df
    else:
        df = df.merge(_df, left_index=True, right_index=True, how='outer')

# Take 12-month rolling mean
df = df.rolling(12).mean()

attrs = {'Return to asset' : {'color' : IMFBLUE},
         'Return to equity' : {'color' : IMFRED}}

# Create Chart object
chart = Chart(df, barcols='Return to asset', rlinecols='Return to equity',
              topxaxis='right',
              barlinewidth=0,
              attrs=attrs,
              title='Bank Profitability 1/',
              subtitle='(Percent, 12M moving average)',
              xrange='2022-01:', yrange=[0, 3], ryrange=[0, 30],
              legend_bottom=0.75, legend_left=0.55)

chart
```

```{python}
#| label: fig1_chart1_gdp
#| fig-cap: "A complicated plot with lines, bars and arrow, text."

infilename = '../data/turkey_page1/fig1_chart1_gdp.csv'
if not os.path.isfile(infilename):
    print('File ' + infilename + ' does not exist')
    sys.exit()
df = pd.read_csv(infilename, index_col=0, parse_dates=[0])
cols = df.columns
linecol = cols[0]
barcols = cols[1:]

# Create Chart object

attrs = {linecol : {'linewidth' : 4,
                    'color' : '#004C97',
                    # 'linestyle' : '--',
                    'marker' : 'o',
                    'markersize' : 10,
                    'markerfacecolor' : 'white',
                    'markeredgewidth' : 4,
                    'markeredgecolor' : '#004C97'},
         'Consumption (private)' : {'color' : '#4B8CAD', 'hatchcolor' : IMFBLUE, 'hatch' : '\\\\'},
         'Consumption (public)' : {'color' : 'white', 'hatch' : '\\\\\\', 'hatchcolor' : IMFBLACK},
         'Net exports' : {'color' : IMFGREY},
         'Inventories/discrepancy' : {'color' : IMFRED},
         'Fixed investment' : {'color' : 'white',
                               'hatch' : '///',
                               'hatchcolor' : IMFGREEN,
                               'hatchwidth' : 10}
         }

chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=linecol, barcols=barcols,
               attrs=attrs,
               topxaxis='left',
               title='Contributions to Real GDP Growth',
               subtitle='(Percent, q/q)',
               xrange='2023Q1:', yrange=[-5, 9.5], ryrange=[-5, 9.5],
               ncol_legend=2)
chart1
```

But with a simple switch of style...

## About this Document

This document was generated by [Quarto](https://quarto.org/){target="_blank"}, a document generation program.
To generate this document yourself, open Command Prompt and do the following.
It is assumed you have R and RStudio installed from the Software Center, which installs Quarto.

```
cd C:\ProgramData\Python3\Lib\site-packages\imfcharts\doc
# If necessary specify full path to quarto
# C:\Program Files\RStudio\resources\app\bin\quarto\bin\quarto.exe render imfcharts.qmd

quarto render imfcharts.qmd
```

and this will generate the HTML and Word output files.

All of the code used to generate the output charts are embedded in the HTML version
of this document, and can be pasted and used directly (as long as the corresponding data is also copied appropriately).
See @sec-quickstart for installation instructions, and for guidance on the `imf_datatools` see [the website](http://datatools/){target="_blank"}.

<!------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------>
# Basic Usage {#sec-basics}

Below basic usage of the `imfcharts` package are shown.
For most purposes it should be possible to create a `Chart` object with a single call to the `Chart()` function
by specifying the data to be charted (a `pandas.DataFrame`) and the columns to be shown as lines, bars, etc.
In some cases further modifications can be made by calling a member function on the `Chart` like `chart.set_xrange([0, 3])`.

## Add Lines {#sec-basics-lines}

The most straightforward use case is to create a line chart, typically a time series chart.
The `Chart()` function will take in a `pandas.DataFrame` and argument `linecols` which can either be a
`str` specifying a column name or an iterable of column names.

```{python}
#| label: lines
#| fig-cap: "A plot with lines"
#| code-fold: show
# Read in data
infilename = '../data/turkey_page1/fig1_chart2_labor.csv'
if not os.path.isfile(infilename):
    print('File ' + infilename + ' does not exist')
    sys.exit()
df = pd.read_csv(infilename, index_col=0, parse_dates=[0])
unemp = df.columns[0]
combined = df.columns[1]

# Create Chart object
chart2 = Chart(df, linecols=unemp, rlinecols=combined,
               title='Labor Utilization',
               subtitle='(Percent)',
               xrange='2023-01:2025-08',
               yrange=[8, 11],
               )
chart2
```

Note that in the above example, the argument `linecols` and `rlinecols` are used
to specify the columns to be plotted on the left and right y-axes, respectively.

To further customize the lines, the `attrs` option can be used which is a `dict` between each
column to be plotted and its attributes. For example, to change the line colors from the defaults, do

```{python}
#| label: lines_color
#| fig-cap: "Specify line colors."
#| code-fold: show

# Create Chart object
chart2 = Chart(df, linecols=unemp, rlinecols=combined,
               attrs={unemp : {'color' : 'magenta'},
	              combined : {'color' : '#00ffff'}},
               title='Labor Utilization',
               subtitle='(Percent)',
               xrange='2023-01:2025-08',
               yrange=[8, 11],
               )
chart2
```

In the above the variable `attrs` is a `dict` with keys corresponding to the line columns.
The colors of each line are specified with the key `color` and corresponding color.

Colors can be specified as

- A valid `matplotlib` color (see [List of named colors](https://matplotlib.org/stable/gallery/color/named_colors.html){target="_blank"}
- A color hex code specified as a `str` with format "#RRGGBB" where each of RR, GG, BB are hexadecimal values from "00" to "FF".
- An RGBA string of the form (r, g, b, t) where r, g, b are values between 0 - 1 and t is the transparency, also 0 - 1.
- Several colors are defined as `IMFBLUE`, `IMFRED`, `IMFGREEN`, `IMFGREY`, `IMFBLACK` and are imported from `imfcharts`.

The available keys for a column within `attrs` are:

- `linewidth` - Line width of marker
- `linestyle` - A valid `Matplotlib` line style specified [here](https://matplotlib.org/stable/gallery/lines_bars_and_markers/linestyles.html).
  Special implementations such as "imfdash", "imfround" are implemented which mimic Excel styles.
- `dashes`, `dash_capstyle` - Advanced options to specify cap styles.
- `legend` - If `False`, do not show this entry in the legend.
- `marker` - Specify the marker (default no marker) using one of symbols [here](https://matplotlib.org/stable/api/markers_api.html).
  Specifying multiple symbols together like "x|" will combine the symbols in the legend.
- `markersize` - Size of marker
- `markerfacecolor` - Face color of marker
- `markeredgecolor` - Edge color of marker
- `markeredgewidth` - Edge width of marker

A fuller example is below.
```{python}
#| label: lines_all_options
#| fig-cap: "Full example of line options."
#| code-fold: show

attrs = {unemp : {'color' : (0.22, 0.5, 0.9, 0.7),
                  'linestyle' : 'dashed',
		  'marker' : 'x|', # combine markers
		  'markeredgecolor' : 'green',
		  'markersize' : 12,
		  'markeredgewidth' : 2,
                 },
         combined : {'color' : 'salmon',
                     'linestyle' : 'dotted',
                     'legend' : False, # don't show in legend
                     'marker' : 'o',
                     'markersize' : 12,
                     'markerfacecolor' : '#00CCCC',
                     'markeredgecolor' : '#AB2323',
                     'markeredgewidth' : 3,
	            }
	}

# Create Chart object
chart2 = Chart(df, linecols=unemp, rlinecols=combined,
               attrs=attrs,
               title='Labor Utilization',
               subtitle='(Percent)',
               xrange='2023-01:2025-08',
               yrange=[8, 11],
               )
chart2
```

Internally, lines are added with the function `Chart.lines()`, other options for this function are

- `indexcol=None` - By default the input `pandas.DataFrame`'s index is used as the x-axis. If this is a column in the DataFrame, specify using this option.
- `axis='left'` - Specify which y-axis to plot on.
- `linebreaks=False` - If `linebreaks=False`, then connect the lines even if there are NA values.
- `xrange=None` - Specify x-axis range. See @sec-basics-format_axes.
- `debug=False` - Show information when running.

## Add Bars {#sec-basics-bars}

To add bars, use the argument `barcols` in `Chart()`. This can either be a `str` specifying a single column
or an iterable of multiple column names.
An example is shown below.

```{python}
#| label: data_barchart
#| fig-cap: "Generate bar chart data."
dict_series = {'S186NGPC@EMERGEMA' : 'Real GDP growth',
               'S186NCYC@EMERGEMA' : 'Consumption1', # resident household
               'S186NCNC@EMERGEMA' : 'Consumption2', # nonprofit serving household
               'S186NCGC@EMERGEMA' : 'Consumption (public)',
               'S186NFC@EMERGEMA'  : 'Fixed investment',
               'S186NXC@EMERGEMA'  : 'Exports',
               'S186NMC@EMERGEMA'  : 'Imports'}

df = None
for series in dict_series:
    _df = imf_datatools.get_haver_data(series)
    _df.columns = [dict_series[series]]
    if df is None:
        df = _df
    else:
        df = df.merge(_df, left_index=True, right_index=True, how='outer')
df['Consumption (private)'] = df['Consumption1'] + df['Consumption2']
df['Net exports'] = df['Exports'] - df['Imports']
df['Inventories/discrepancy'] = df['Real GDP growth'] - df['Consumption (private)'] - df['Consumption (public)'] - df['Fixed investment'] - df['Net exports']

gdpcol = 'Real GDP growth'
barcols = ['Consumption (private)', 'Consumption (public)', 'Net exports', 'Inventories/discrepancy', 'Fixed investment']
df = df[[gdpcol] + barcols].copy()
# Normalize change by previous GDP value
for barcol in barcols:
    df[barcol] = 100. * df[barcol].diff() / df[gdpcol].shift()
# Calculate %pop for GDP
df[gdpcol] = imf_datatools.dataframe_utilities.calc_pop(df[gdpcol])
```

```{python}
#| label: barchart
#| fig-cap: "Example bar chart."
#| code-fold: show
linecol = cols[0]
barcols = cols[1:]

# Create Chart object
chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=linecol, barcols=barcols,
               topxaxis='left',
               title='Contributions to Real GDP Growth',
               subtitle='(Percent, q/q)',
               xrange='2023Q1:', yrange=[-5, 9.5], ryrange=[-5, 9.5],
               ncol_legend=2)
chart1
```

Similar to plotting lines, an option `attrs` can be passed in to specify the attributes of each bar.
The default is to follow the color scheme that is specified in the current style.

The available options in `attrs` for bars is as follows:

- `color` - The color of bars for this column.
- `width` - Specify width of bars. If time series, in units of days.
- `offset` - Additional offset for bars. If time series, in units of days.
- `edgecolor` - Edge colors of bars.
- `hatchcolor` - Specify colors for any hatches
- `hatch` - Specify a valid hatch specified [here](https://matplotlib.org/stable/gallery/shapes_and_collections/hatch_style_reference.html)
- `hatchwidth` - Specify hatch bar width
- `edgecolor` - Overwrites for each column what is passed through same option in `Charts()` or `bars()`
- `legend` - If `False`, do not add to legend.
- `barcolors` - Specify color of individual colors of bars using the index of the data. See example below.

Note that the default is to have the edges of bars in black. This color can be modfiied with `baredgecolor` for each column,
and to remove use the option `barlinewidth=0` when calling `Chart()` or `Chart.bars()`.

An example with customized bar `attrs` is below.

```{python}
#| label: barchart_options
#| fig-cap: "Example bar chart."
#| code-fold: show

# Create Chart object
attrs = {linecol : {'linewidth' : 4,
                    'color' : '#004C97',
                    # 'linestyle' : '--',
                    'marker' : 'o',
                    'markersize' : 10,
                    'markerfacecolor' : 'white',
                    'markeredgewidth' : 4,
                    'markeredgecolor' : '#004C97'},
         'Consumption (private)' : {'color' : '#4B8CAD', 'barhatchcolor' : IMFBLUE, 'barhatch' : '\\\\'},
         'Consumption (public)' : {'color' : 'white', 'barhatch' : '\\\\\\', 'barhatchcolor' : IMFBLACK},
         'Net exports' : {'color' : IMFGREY},
         'Inventories/discrepancy' : {'color' : IMFRED},
         'Fixed investment' : {'color' : 'white',
                               'barhatch' : '///',
                               'barhatchcolor' : IMFGREEN,
                               'barhatchwidth' : 10}
         }

chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=linecol, barcols=barcols,
               attrs=attrs,
               topxaxis='left',
               title='Contributions to Real GDP Growth',
               subtitle='(Percent, q/q)',
               xrange='2023Q1:', yrange=[-5, 9.5], ryrange=[-5, 9.5],
               ncol_legend=2)
chart1
```

Internally, bar charts are created with the function `chart.bars()`. Other options for this function are

- `indexcol=None` -By default the input `pandas.DataFrame`'s index is used as the x-axis. If this is a column in the DataFrame, specify using this option.
- `axis='left'` - Specify which y-axis to plot on.
- `stack=True` - Specify whether to stack bars. If `True`, all bars are stacked on top of each other (default), otherwise they are staggered.
- `total_barwidth=None` - When not plotting bars as a time series, specify the width of each bar, default is 0.9.
- `linewidth=None` - Width of edges of bars.
- `edgecolor='black'` - Edge color for bars. Can be overwritten for individual columns by `attrs`.
- `xrange=None` - Specify x-axis range. See @sec-basics-format_axes.
- `debug=False` - Show information when running.

## Add Lines and Rectangles {#sec-basics-lines_rects}

    def hline(self, y, xrange=None, coordinates='data', color='red', linewidth=1, linestyle='-', alpha=1, dashes=None, dash_capstyle=None,
    def vline(self, x, yrange=None, coordinates='data', width=1, color='red', linewidth=1, linestyle='-', alpha=1, dashes=None, dash_capstyle=None):
    def hrect(self, y0, y1, width=1, linecolor='red', fillcolor=None, dash='-', opacity=1, text=''):
    def vrect(self, y0, y1, width=1, linecolor='red', fillcolor=None, dash='-', opacity=1, text=''):
    def text(self, x, y, text='', xycoords='data', color='black',
    def arrow(self, head=(0, 0), tail=(1, 1),

Add hlines
Add vlines

Add hrect
Add vrect

## Additional Options{#sec-basics-additional}

### Adding Titles, Subtitles, axis titles.

The `Chart()` function has arguments `title` and `subtitle` which allow specifying titles and subtitles, respectively.
Internally these call `Chart.title()` and `Chart.subtitle()`.

Axes titles are set within `Chart()` with arguments `xtitle` and `yttile`, together with `xtitlesize`, `ytitlesize`.
Internally these call `Chart.set_xtitle(xtitle, xtitlesize)` and `Chart.set_ytitle(ytitle, ytitlesize)`.

                 xtickfontsize=14, ytickfontsize=14,
                 xticklength=None, yticklength=None,
                 xtickangle=0,
                 nxticks=7,
                 xformat='auto',
                 dict_legend=None,
                 dict_xaxis=None,
                 dict_yaxis=None,
                 # style
                 style='default',

                 xrange=None, yrange=None, ryrange=None,
                 xmargins='auto',
                 width=10, height=6,
                 topxaxis='left',
                 hlines=[], vlines=[], hrects=[], vrects=[],

### Legend options.
                 ncol_legend=1,
                 legend_spacing=0.5,
                 legend_fontsize=14,
                 legend_header='',
                 legend_left=0.04, legend_bottom=0.85, legend_width=0.70, legend_height=0.15,
                 legend_mode='expand',
                 show_legend=True,
    def update_legend(self,
    def set_show_legend(self, option):

### Show, save `Chart`.
    def save(self, filename, dpi=250):
    def show(self, debug=False):

### Show left or right x-axis on top.
    `topxaxis`.
    
    def set_top_xaxis(self, topxaxis='left'):

### Showing information.

To show internal debugging messages, set `debug=True`.


## Formatting Axis and Setting Ranges {#sec-basics-format_axes}

format axes

    def set_xrange(self, xrange, margins='auto', debug=False):
    def set_yrange(self, yrange):
    def set_ryrange(self, ryrange):

    def set_xticks(self, size=None, length=None, angle=0):
    def set_yticks(self, size=None, length=None):
    def set_nxticks(self, nxticks):
                def quarter_formatter(x, pos):
    def set_date_format(self, xformat='auto', debug=False):

## Miscellaneous Topics

Set which y-axis comes on top with chart.set_top_xaxis('left')

By default, if NA values are in a column, the chart will connect all line segments.
This may be misleading though as there is no data for some parts of the line.
To show breaks in the line charts, set linebreaks=True.

<!------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------>
# Switching Styles {#sec-styles}

The `imfcharts` package is available with several pre-defined styles which will automatically set the style to be that for IMF Article IV reports etc.
One of the main purposes of this library is to allow switching between styles to be easy.
By doing this charts can be customized not just for existing styles but can be reused for any future changes in style.

When the `imfcharts` library is imported, it will automatically copy the available style files where `matplotlib` can find them.
To do this copying manually, do

```
from imfcharts import *
install_styles(force=True)
```

which will copy the style files to `matplotlib.get_configdir() + '/stylelib'` which is typically `C:\Users\[user]\.matlotlib\stylelib`
on a Fund laptop.

The command

```
# This is just returning plt.style.available
show_styles()
```

will show all available styles, including ones that are included from `matplotlib` and other libraries.

To switch styles, call the command

```{python}
#| eval: false
# This is just a wrapper around plt.style.use() with some guardrails
set_style('imf-articleiv')
```

To temporarily switch styles, do the following.

```{python}
#| label: get_data_ca


# Get data
cacol = 'Current account'
dict_series = {'N186BC@EMERGEMA' : cacol,
               'N186IX3@EMERGEMA' : 'export_fuel',
	       'N186IM3@EMERGEMA' : 'import_fuel',
               'N186BGXN@EMERGEMA' : 'export_gold',
	       'N186BGMN@EMERGEMA' : 'import_gold'}
df = None
for series in dict_series:
    _df = imf_datatools.get_haver_data(series)
    _df.columns = [dict_series[series]]
    if df is None:
        df = _df
    else:
        df = df.merge(_df, left_index=True, right_index=True, how='outer')
df['Excluding fuel'] = df[cacol] - (df['export_fuel'] - df['import_fuel'])
df['Excluding fuel & gold'] = df[cacol] - (df['export_fuel'] - df['import_fuel']) - (df['export_gold'] - df['import_gold'])
df['Excluding gold'] = df[cacol] - (df['export_gold'] - df['import_gold'])
df.drop(['export_fuel', 'import_fuel', 'export_gold', 'import_gold'], axis=1, inplace=True)

# Take 12-month moving sum and divide by 1000
df = df.rolling(12).sum() / 1000
```

```{python}
#| label: style_comparison
#| fig-cap: "Switching styles."
#| code-fold: show
# Create Chart object with default IMF Article IV style
set_style('imf-articleiv')
chart = Chart(df, linecols=df.columns,
              title='Current Account', subtitle='(Billions USD, 12mms)',
              xrange='2022-01:', yrange=[-95, 60],
              legend_bottom=0.28, legend_left=0.60, height=4)
chart
```

```{python}
#| label: style_comparison2
#| fig-cap: "Switching styles."
#| code-fold: show
# Create Chart object with Fund Guide style
set_style('fund-guide')
chart2 = Chart(df, linecols=df.columns,
               title='Current Account', subtitle='(Billions USD, 12mms)',
               xrange='2022-01:', yrange=[-95, 60],
               legend_bottom=0.28, legend_left=0.60, height=4)
chart2
```

It is also possible to switch styles temporarily for a single chart:



<!------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------>
# Usage in R {#sec-R}

How to use in R


<!------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------>
# Usage in Stata {#sec-stata}

How to use in Stata


<!------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------>
# Usage in AIDA {#sec-aida}

How to use in AIDA
